proposal.reasoning: This is the initial exploration phase with no prior data, requiring diverse sampling across the parameter space to identify promising regions. I propose 4 distinct parameter sets (forks) that systematically explore different physical regimes: (1) Fork 1 tests high cohesion (strong epsilon) and high polarity (long dummy bonds, high charges) expecting increased density and surface tension; (2) Fork 2 tests weak cohesion and low polarity to establish baseline behavior; (3) Fork 3 tests intermediate values with maximally extended geometry (wide angles) to maximize dipole separation; (4) Fork 4 tests intermediate cohesion with compact geometry (acute angles) and moderate polarity. This covers extremes and intermediates of Lennard-Jones parameters (epsilon, rmin), electrostatic parameters (charges, dummy bond lengths), and geometric parameters (angles), enabling identification of which parameter classes most strongly influence the target properties (density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, dipole moment). All constraints are respected: dummy bond force constants >100 kcal/mol/Å², DUN charges equal and opposite to DUP charges, and dummy bond lengths remain below sigma/2 of core beads.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Changing Lennard-Jones epsilon from weak (-0.4 to -0.6 kcal/mol) to strong (-1.2 kcal/mol) will modulate cohesive energy, directly impacting Heat of Vaporization and Surface Tension (stronger cohesion increases both). Varying rmin (2.5-3.0 Å) controls packing efficiency and density (smaller rmin increases density). Modifying dummy bead bond lengths (0.4-1.3 Å) and charges (0.15-0.45 e) controls the dipole moment magnitude (longer separation and higher charges increase dipole). Varying angles (100-175°) changes the molecular geometry and dipole orientation. Specifically, Fork 1 (high cohesion/polarity) should maximize density, HVap, and surface tension; Fork 2 (low cohesion/polarity) should minimize these; Fork 3 (linear geometry) should maximize dipole moment through extended conformation; Fork 4 (compact geometry) tests steric effects.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will identify which parameter combinations produce liquid-state stability while matching target thermodynamic properties (Density ~1.095 g/cm³, HVap ~12.6 kcal/mol, Surface Tension ~42 mN/m) and electronic properties (Dipole ~3.96 D). The exploration will reveal whether the system is more sensitive to dispersive interactions (epsilon), packing (rmin), or electrostatics (charges/geometry), guiding refinement in subsequent iterations.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 4 parameter sets in parallel molecular dynamics simulations, measuring the resulting thermodynamic and electronic properties to determine which fork(s) produce the most accurate target values and stable liquid behavior.

The dipole moment is also overestimated due to excessive charge separation. proposal.reasoning: The diagnostic report reveals a critical shift in optimization needs: the system now exhibits under-cohesion (density -16.5%, HVap -19%) rather than over-cohesion, indicating the previous aggressive epsilon reductions overshot the target. With a stuck counter of 4 approaching the threshold of 5, I must implement a strategic reversal to strengthen intermolecular attractions while optimizing dipole moment. The 4 forks systematically explore: (1) High epsilon (-0.55) with maximum dummy bond lengths (1.5Å) and increased charges (0.20) to maximally boost density, HVap, and dipole simultaneously; (2) Moderate epsilon (-0.50) with reduced rmin (4.95) to test if size reduction can compensate for moderate cohesion increase; (3) Conservative epsilon (-0.48) with aggressive charge increase (0.22) and maximum bond lengths to isolate the dipole improvement mechanism; (4) Balanced high epsilon (-0.52) with intermediate geometry to test stability boundaries. All forks avoid crash regions by maintaining rmin ≥ 4.95 and rminby2 ≥ 2.8, respect the 1.5Å bond length ceiling, and reduce angle force constants to 30-35 to address surface tension variance.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Increasing LJ epsilon magnitude from -0.45 toward -0.48 to -0.55 strengthens intermolecular attractions, directly correcting the 16.5% density deficit and 19% HVap underestimation caused by insufficient cohesive forces. Simultaneously, increasing dummy bead charges from 0.18 to 0.20-0.22 and extending bond lengths toward the 1.5Å maximum (while remaining well below sigma/2 = 2.55-2.8Å) increases charge separation to boost the dipole moment toward 3.96 D. Slight reduction in CGD2_SUL_rmin (to 4.95-5.0) enhances packing density without entering crash regions (rmin < 4.9).
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will simultaneously improve all three deficient properties: density and HVap will increase due to stronger LJ attractions and tighter packing, while the dipole moment will increase through enhanced charge separation. Surface tension, currently well-tuned, will be monitored for stability with reduced angle force constants (30-35) to minimize variance.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating 4 parameter sets spanning epsilon values from -0.48 to -0.55 combined with systematic charge (0.19-0.22) and bond length (1.48-1.50Å) variations to identify the optimal trade-off between cohesive energy and dipole magnitude.

proposal.reasoning: Parent 1 (Score: 35.15) outperformed Parent 2 (Score: 36.85) with weaker LJ interactions (epsilon=-0.45 vs -0.48) and longer dummy bonds (1.45Å vs 1.4Å) that better address the over-cohesion and low dipole moment issues. I implemented a strategic crossover that inherits the superior CGD2-SUL cross-interaction parameters from Parent 1 to reduce excessive cohesion, while incorporating Parent 2's SUL self-interaction parameters (epsilon=-0.48, rmin=2.95) to test if stronger SUL-SUL attraction combined with weaker CGD2-SUL cross-interaction improves density without worsening surface tension. Parent 1's longer dummy bonds (1.45Å) are retained to maintain dipole moment improvements. For angle parameters, I mixed sources: taking the DUP-CGD2-SUL angle from Parent 2 (ktheta=25, more flexible) and the CGD2-SUL-DUN angle from Parent 1 (ktheta=40, stiffer) to explore geometric flexibility effects. Given stuck_counter=4 (<5), conservative mutations of 3-4% were applied: CGD2_SUL_rmin increased to 5.253 (3%) to slightly increase excluded volume, and DUP_CGD2_SUL_ktheta increased to 26.0 (4%) to test slightly stiffer angles while staying within genetic boundaries.
